text: For any nerve-preserving sub-siphoid rhabrolysis thymectomy in a child with mediastinal lymphoma, Minimally invasive thymectomy in children is technically challenging because of the small sizes of thoracic cavity and the agenous structures. We report a case of a successful resection of a large mediastinal tumor in case of burning nerve with the use of sub-siphoid RUB-assisted thymectomy in a child.

A thirteen-year-old boy was referred to our hospital for the evaluation of asymptomatic bulging mass-like opacity at left anterior mediastinum, which was detected on preschool checkup. CT scans revealed a six-point-seven centimeter size multiloculated cystic mass. An MRI demonstrated a dense, solid lesion with peripheral septations, suggesting benign cystic pathology. Tumor markers were also negative.

Under the presumptive diagnosis of cystic dimoma, we planned minimally invasive operation for this pediatric patient. There seems to be too small available space in the left thoracic cavity. Subsyphoid robotic cystic thymectomy was planned instead of left transplural approach.

The patient is positioned, and a four-centimeter longitudinal subsyphoid incision is made to enter the chest cavity. The substernal area is dissected using electric cutters under thoracoscopic guidance. A glove port is placed at the incision site, and CO₂ gas is insufflated to enhance exposure of the thoracic cavity. The first robotic arm is positioned at the right midclavicular line, fifth intercostal space; the second arm at the left anterior axillary line, third intercostal space; and the third arm at the left midclavicular line, sixth intercostal space. Robotic dissection begins in the retro-sternal area, with visible intramammary vessels and both phrenic nerves using the sub-siphoid approach.
The surgeon performs dissection of the mediastinal contents, sweeping them up while maintaining a plane just superficial to the pericardium.

The surgeon continues dissection at the right cervical poles of the thymus using the subcyphoid approach, followed by dissection of the left cervical pole. A mass containing mixed cystic and semi-solid lesions is identified, appearing to encase the left mammary vein, nominic vein, and left phrenic nerve.
The surgeon separates the nomid vein from the tumor, controlling the draining branch with hemoclips. The left phrenic nerve is identified, showing encasement by the tumor.
The surgeon identifies the vagus nerve positioned behind the mass and performs sharp dissection to separate the tumor from surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon uses robotic dissection to carefully separate the phrenic nerve from the tumor. The tumor is opened over the phrenic nerve, allowing for its preservation with minimal disruption to the tumor tissue.

The surgeon performs an en bloc removal of the tumor, thymus, perithymic fat, and surrounding tissue, extending from the thoracic inlet to the diaphragm. The excised tumor is retrieved through the subxiphoid working window.

The final pathological findings were consistent with lymphangioma, showing lymphoid tissue and cystic lesions lined by flattened epithelium with positive staining for vascular endothelial marker CD-XXXI.
The patient fared well postoperatively without any signs of diaphragmatic palsy and discharged home at the third postoperative day.

Subcyphoid RoboCystic Zymectomy is a novel and effective approach in pediatric patients with large anterior mediastinal tumor. Robotic manipulation may help minimize surgical trauma to the surrounding tissues and maximize meticulous control during the tumor dissection. transopsyphoid approach also provides excellent view and better access to the surgical area. These advantages further expand the role of robots in mediastinal tumor resections for the pediatric population.
